structure_blocks.text: When a security requirement is specified in the Security Requirements Check List (SRCL), clients must verify that the contractor and their personnel maintain valid security statuses throughout the contract. This is essential before they access any protected or classified information, assets or sites. For procurements using standing offers or supply arrangements, clients must coordinate with the directorate responsible for the Contract Security Program (CSP) to ensure the necessary security levels are met before issuing call-ups and contracts.

Buyers are required to communicate the security status of the organization to the client at the time of contract award and must update the client on any changes in security statuses identified by the CSP throughout the duration of the contract.

raw_text:
Security

When a security requirement is specified in the Security Requirements Check List (SRCL), clients must verify that the contractor and their personnel maintain valid security statuses throughout the contract. This is essential before they access any protected or classified information, assets or sites. For procurements using standing offers or supply arrangements, clients must coordinate with the directorate responsible for the Contract Security Program (CSP) to ensure the necessary security levels are met before issuing call-ups and contracts.

Clients are responsible for confirming that everyone accessing the work sites has the appropriate security clearance. Buyers are required to communicate the security status of the organization to the client at the time of contract award and must update the client on any changes in security statuses identified by the CSP throughout the duration of the contract.

Verifying compliance with security requirements

During the contract term clients must ensure that all contractor and subcontractor personnel who need access to classified or protected information, assets or sensitive sites, including government systems and cloud service portals, are recognized as contract affiliates and have verified security statuses in accordance with the CSP . Buyers will provide necessary assistance with this process as required.

If a buyer learns that a subcontractor requires access to classified or protected information or sites, they must immediately consult with the directorate responsible for the CSP . This step ensures that the SRCL s for subcontracts are submitted and the subcontractor(s) meet the security criteria.

Compliance and enforcement with industrial security requirements

Consult the Policy on the Contract Security Program (accessible only on the Government of Canada network) to address and manage non-compliance with security requirements during a contract's duration. It gives PSPC and buyers structured procedures to address the situation progressively and systematically.

The Industrial Security Sector (ISS) actively monitors compliance and communicates updates to buyers, clients and other relevant parties at key points throughout the enforcement process. Should a security clearance be revoked, the ADM of the respective branch is notified to ensure all related procurement authorities are informed.

The enforcement of policy is triggered when non-compliance is confirmed and involves 3 escalating levels of action. Buyers must document their file at each level to ensure a clear record of actions and communications.

Level 1 Warning

The ISS issues a warning letter to the organization that lists the corrective actions required. This letter is shared with you and the client, who should then consider potential actions if compliance is not achieved. Discussions should involve legal counsel. The organization is given 30 calendar days to comply.

Level 2 Second warning

If non-compliance persists the ISS sends a second letter restating the corrective measures and advising of the potential revocation of clearances. You and client must assess the impact of continued non-compliance on the contract. Involvement of legal counsel is strongly advised. An additional 30-day compliance period is provided.

Level 3 Revocation of security status

A final letter advising of the revocation of security clearance is sent should non-compliance continue. At this stage, you and client must conduct a risk assessment to determine further actions. These actions may include:

amending the contract to align with the security capabilities of the contractor along with necessary legal consultations

considering contract termination for default and applying appropriate corrective measure in accordance with policy

Note: Where an amendment occurs there is a need to establish measures to address the portion of the work the contractor cannot undertake due to not holding the required security status. Any amendments need to be discussed with the legal counsel and take into consideration generally accepted contract management practices under the Canadian International Trade Tribunal.

When a security requirement is specified in the Security Requirements Check List (SRCL), clients must verify that the contractor and their personnel maintain valid security statuses throughout the contract. This is essential before they access any protected or classified information, assets or sites. For procurements using standing offers or supply arrangements, clients must coordinate with the directorate responsible for the Contract Security Program (CSP) to ensure the necessary security levels are met before issuing call-ups and contracts.

Clients are responsible for confirming that everyone accessing the work sites has the appropriate security clearance. Buyers are required to communicate the security status of the organization to the client at the time of contract award and must update the client on any changes in security statuses identified by the CSP throughout the duration of the contract.

Verifying compliance with security requirements

During the contract term clients must ensure that all contractor and subcontractor personnel who need access to classified or protected information, assets or sensitive sites, including government systems and cloud service portals, are recognized as contract affiliates and have verified security statuses in accordance with the CSP . Buyers will provide necessary assistance with this process as required.

It gives PSPC and buyers structured procedures to address the situation progressively and systematically.

The Industrial Security Sector (ISS) actively monitors compliance and communicates updates to buyers, clients and other relevant parties at key points throughout the enforcement process. Should a security clearance be revoked, the ADM of the respective branch is notified to ensure all related procurement authorities are informed.

The enforcement of policy is triggered when non-compliance is confirmed and involves 3 escalating levels of action. Buyers must document their file at each level to ensure a clear record of actions and communications.
